parts: skull, jaw, teeth, tibia, vertebrae, rib, ankel and foot, other unidentified bones
dispersal: Some of the bones sent to the Bishop of Grenoble for identification; Some sent to the University in Montpellier; Mazurier toured with the bones including to Paris, Fontainebleau Palace, northern France, England, Flanders, and Germany

parts: skull, teeth, shoulder blade, limb bones
Head and leg, incl. soft tissue, teeth
dispersal: Pieces taken by Ides to Turukhansk, possibly also to China, 1693

recs.geonote: Longitude direction corrected W→E — the Indigirka is in NE Siberia.
dispersal: Molar tooth purchased at auction by Sloane, from Johan van Segvelt, Director of the Dutch West India Company; Segvelt obtained it from the cabinet of curiosities of Nicholaes Witsen; molar currently resides at the NHM in London

recs.geonote: Alazeya River, lower course, NE Yakutia (approximate).

dispersal: Piece of skin and hair (uncertain) to Stuttgart, 1816;

recs.geonote: “Polar Sea” — location too general to map.
dispersal: Sample of fur given by Potapov to Tilesius en route to Kamchatka, 1805

parts: Skeleton, hair, bones, soft tissue, brain
dispersal: Specimens first taken to Obdorsk, then to Moscow before going to St. Petersburg

parts: Skeleton only, bones, rotted, with an an organic materialin the soil that may have originated from the animal's soft tissue
Eight tusks, molars, thigh bones, ribs
skull tusks and a quantity of hair and wool
dispersal: Specimens taken to Haslar Hospital; tusks kept at Haslar Museum, in Porstmouth, UK. When Haslar Museum closed in 2007, most of its collection was acquired by the British Museum in London.
Forwarded by the discoverer, Robert Campbell, to the British Museum in 1852
Khitrovo, a missionary, first sent the foot the Archbishop Nil (probably Isakovich), who then donated it to the Museum of Geographical Society
Samples studied by Famintsyn, St. Petersburg, mid-to-late 1880s

The head, along with the tusks and some of the skin and hair, was first found by a Yurak who brought it to Dudinka; Schmidt attempted to find the rest of the mammoth but could find only scraps, which he brought to St. Petersburg
The specimen was sited by a Yakutsk trader, but the RGS made no effort to retrieve it

"A specimen of the 'hard tallow" containing "numerous dired muscular fibres" is preserved at the US National Museum" ie the Smithsonian; the local "Eskimos" were also reportedly using the mammoth fat to grease their boats (according to Dall);
Left hind leg muscle tissue to Smithsonian, 1922

parts: Skeleton, fur, soft tissue, trunk, skin, ears, lips, penis, organs, hoofs,
dispersal: Samples of hide and hair sent to Paris, 1910; skull, left tusk, upper lip, left eye, left ear, skin from the hip, tail, penis, meat, all four feet, hair sent to St. Petersburg, 1910.

Radiocarbon Laboratory, Silesian University of Technology; Poland Geological Institute; Russian Academy of Sciences; Poznan Radiocarbon laboratory; Foundation of the Adam Mickiewicz University, Poland
Quackenbush reports that some of the bones had already been accumulated by a collector

parts: right innominate bone, femur, tibia and fibula, four of the small foot bones, lower jaw ith teeth, two tusks, fragments of skull, six thoracic vertebrae, several caudal vertebrae, end of the tail with skin and hair, hair, skin, soft tissue

dispersal: Samples of fur sent to the British Museum at some point between 1908-1926 **Follow up on Digby_1926
Yenisei museum sent samples of fur and tissues, 1908
Carcass looted by wild animals and "natives" before  collection could take place
No attempt was made to collec this specimen
The skull was sold to the British Museum by Siberian ivory traders in 1923; there doesn't seem to be a record of whether or not this was the whole or only a part of the find.
Specimen sent to Museum of Natural History; Cast was made and is on display at Gallery of Alaska, Fairbanks

parts: Head, tusks, flesh, wool, vertebrae, ribs, skin, right forearm bones
skull, tusks, parts of skeleton, hair
dispersal: Hair sent to researchers at CSIRO in Canberra, Australia, 1970; hair also sent to Gifu University, Japan, 1971; skeleton

Femur bone kept in John Hebior's basement from 1979-1994; skeleton kept in Kenosha 1994-2007; aquired by Milwaukee Public Museum 2007; displayed in New York 2010

dispersal: Stored first at Salekhard, 1988; Displayed in Manchester on loan, 2014
